Samson's downward spiral begins not with a dramatic rebellion but with something that looks almost ordinary—he sees a woman and wants her. His parents recognize the danger immediately: she is a Philistine, one of the very people oppressing Israel, and the law explicitly forbids such unions. Their objection is not prudish but faithful, grounded in God's covenant commands. Yet Samson's response cuts them off with a demand wrapped in desire: "she is pleasing to my eyes." He will not hear them. Notice what the narrator tells us—this whole mess is actually part of God's plan to strike against the Philistines. This is a stunning detail. God is at work, but His work here involves Samson's willfulness, his refusal to listen to his parents, his pursuit of what his eyes want rather than what his God requires. God will use even Samson's sin as an instrument against His enemies. That does not make the sin right. It makes it more troubling.

The power of the Spirit comes upon Samson, and he tears a lion apart with his bare hands. This should arrest him. This should make him reckon with the reality of God working through him. Instead, he keeps it secret and continues toward the woman. He eats honey from the lion's carcass without telling his parents—a small act, but it reveals the pattern: Samson receives from God, encounters divine power, and then hides the truth. He is experiencing God's strength while living in darkness, telling no one what he has seen or done. The feast arrives, and Samson poses a riddle to the Philistine men, wagering fine clothes on whether they can solve it. The riddle itself points to what only Samson knows—the lion he killed, now alive with bees, death made fertile. It is clever and cruel, a game with his identity woven into it.

When the Philistine men cannot solve the riddle, they threaten to burn Samson's new wife and her father alive. She weeps and pleads with Samson until, exhausted by her pressure on the seventh day, he gives her the answer. She tells her people. Samson discovers he has been betrayed—the men announce the solution, and his rage ignites. The Spirit of the Lord comes upon him again, and he kills thirty Philistines and gives their clothes to those who solved the riddle. But notice what happens next: he returns to his father's house "burning with anger," and his wife is given to another man. The chapter ends in dissolution. Samson's lust brought him a wife through deception and pressure, and now she is gone, given away. He has proven himself strong enough to tear lions and kill men, yet he could not keep the woman his eyes desired. He has won his wager and lost his marriage. The Spirit empowered him for a blow against Philistine oppression, but Samson's eyes, his desires, and his secrets have tangled everything into chaos. God's purposes will be served, but at the cost of truth abandoned and relationships destroyed.
